critic_raw:
This transformer-based depression severity prediction design is carefully specified but contains a label construction problem, a clinical notes contamination risk, and a baseline feature asymmetry that undermine the core claim.

The label is defined as 'the most recently recorded patient-reported depression severity score (e.g., PHQ-9) within 30 days of a clinical encounter.' This definition means the label is a PHQ-9 score that was recorded during the same or an immediately prior encounter as the one being used to extract clinical notes features. Clinical notes from that encounter will frequently contain the actual PHQ-9 score, its interpretation, or the clinician's response to it (e.g., 'PHQ-9 score of 14, moderate depression, medication adjusted accordingly'). The transformer, trained on these notes, can trivially learn to extract or recall the numeric score from the note text rather than predicting it from clinical signals — a form of label leakage through the text modality. The design claims 'no feature is constructed that encodes the label' but this does not prevent the raw text from containing the label numerically.

The baseline model uses 'count of depression-related diagnoses in the prior 12 months' and 'current use of antidepressant medication' as features, but no prior PHQ-9 scores as features. The most predictive feature for a patient's current PHQ-9 score is almost certainly their prior PHQ-9 score — depression follows a chronic course with substantial autocorrelation. By excluding prior PHQ-9 scores from the baseline, the design sets up a strawman comparison: the transformer can potentially recover PHQ-9 history from note text ('patient reports PHQ-9 was 16 last month'), while the baseline is denied this information in structured form.

The patient-level temporal split (all records for a patient assigned to one split) prevents patient-level leakage but creates a covariate shift problem: patients who receive a PHQ-9 assessment in the test period (year 5) may systematically differ from training patients. Depression measurement practices evolve — if PHQ-9 administration became more common over the study period (common in many health systems), year 5 patients would include more recently diagnosed, potentially less severe cases than training patients. This is the opposite of temporal generalization and would inflate the appearance of model accuracy.

The secondary ablation ('transformer without notes') is noted as a means to isolate architectural effects from note information, but this ablation is designated as secondary, not primary. If the transformer without notes outperforms ridge regression with comparable feature sets, the improvement is from architectural capacity rather than clinical note content — which would directly refute the primary hypothesis. The design should pre-specify what this ablation result implies for the main conclusion.
all_issues_raised: Clinical notes from the same or immediately prior encounter will frequently contain the PHQ-9 score or its clinical interpretation in text form; the transformer can learn to extract this numeric value from notes, constituting label leakage through the text modality that the design's feature exclusion check does not prevent.

The baseline is denied prior PHQ-9 scores as structured features even though depression severity has strong temporal autocorrelation; the transformer can implicitly recover this history from note text, creating an asymmetric information advantage that artificially inflates the apparent multi-modal gain.

If PHQ-9 administration increased over the 5-year study period (common in many health systems), year 5 test patients will include more newly diagnosed, less severe cases, creating a systematic covariate shift between training and test populations that would inflate accuracy on the test set while obscuring true temporal generalization.

The transformer-without-notes ablation is designated as secondary and its implications for the main hypothesis are not pre-specified; if this ablation shows comparable improvement to the full transformer, the improvement is attributable to architectural capacity rather than clinical note content, directly refuting the stated hypothesis without a planned conclusion.
all_issues_adjudicated: Clinical notes from the same or immediately prior encounter will frequently contain the PHQ-9 score or its clinical interpretation in text form; the transformer can learn to extract this numeric value from notes, constituting label leakage through the text modality that the design's feature exclusion check does not prevent.

The baseline is denied prior PHQ-9 scores as structured features even though depression severity has strong temporal autocorrelation; the transformer can implicitly recover this history from note text, creating an asymmetric information advantage that artificially inflates the apparent multi-modal gain.

If PHQ-9 administration increased over the 5-year study period (common in many health systems), year 5 test patients will include more newly diagnosed, less severe cases, creating a systematic covariate shift between training and test populations that would inflate accuracy on the test set while obscuring true temporal generalization.

The transformer-without-notes ablation is designated as secondary and its implications for the main hypothesis are not pre-specified; if this ablation shows comparable improvement to the full transformer, the improvement is attributable to architectural capacity rather than clinical note content, directly refuting the stated hypothesis without a planned conclusion.
